name: Calisthenics
details: A form of strength training that uses primarily bodyweight exercises and minimal equipment.

name: Isometric Hold
details: An isometric hold is a type of strength training where you contract a muscle and hold a static position without changing the muscle's length or moving the surrounding joints. Instead of lifting and lowering a weight, your muscles generate tension simply by remaining completely still.

name: Shoulder (scapular) Protraction
details: Shoulder (scapular) protraction is the movement where your shoulder blades slide forward and away from your spine, spreading them apart. Think of it as reaching, punching, or rounding your shoulders forward

name: Concentric Phase
details: The concentric phase is the "lifting" or active portion of an exercise where a muscle contracts, shortens, and generates enough force to overcome an external resistance (like gravity or a weight).

name: Eccentric Phase
details: The eccentric phase of a movement is the lowering or lengthening portion of an exercise where a muscle contracts while being stretched by an external load or gravity. It acts as a braking mechanism to control the speed of the descent, such as slowly lowering a bicep curl or squatting down

name: Shoulder (scapular) Depression
details: Scapular depression is the downward, or caudal, movement of your shoulder blades (scapulae) away from your ears
linkTerms: pull your shoulders away from your ears

name: Triceps
details: Triceps (triceps brachii) refers to the large muscle located at the back of the upper arm

name: Push-up Position
details:
Place your hands on the floor, about shoulder-width apart.
Keep your arms straight (but not locked).
Position your feet together or slightly apart.
Your body should form a straight line from head to heels. Engage your core and glutes to avoid sagging hips

name: Tighten Your Core
details: To "tighten your core" (or engage it) means to activate the muscles in your midsection—including your abs, obliques, and lower back—to create a sturdy "corset" for your spine. It stabilizes your body during movement, improves posture, and protects your lower back from injury

name: Posterior Pelvic Tilt
details: A posterior pelvic tilt is when your pelvis rotates backward, causing your tailbone to tuck underneath you and flattening the natural curve of your lower back

name: Anterior Pelvic Tilt
details: An anterior pelvic tilt is when your pelvis rotates forward, causing your tailbone to stick out and round the natural curve of your lower back

name: Keeping Tension
details: "keeping tension" means continuously flexing and engaging the target muscle throughout the entire movement. It prevents the muscle from "resting" at the top or bottom of a repetition, forcing it to work harder and increasing the overall mechanical stress (Time Under Tension) needed to stimulate growth

name: Straddle
details: Keeping your legs apart rather than together
